WA3872 Lab Guide

Introduction to Command Line, Git, and IDEs for Leaders

Evergreen Insurance Quote Page — First Look

Lab 1

In this lab, you will open the Evergreen Insurance Quote Page in Visual Studio Code, run it in your browser, make a single one-line change, and see how VS Code surfaces that change in its Source Control panel.

You are not writing code this week. The goal is to get comfortable with the three tools your engineers use every day — the command line, Git, and VS Code — while looking at a realistic insurance web app.

 Tip

The Evergreen app is shared across every week of this course. You’ll come back to it in HTML week, CSS week, and beyond. For this week, just get oriented.

1.1. A Note About Keyboard Shortcuts

Your lab VM runs inside your browser. Some keyboard shortcuts (like Ctrl+Shift+X, Ctrl+S, Ctrl+F) may not always reach the VM — sometimes your browser catches them first. If a shortcut doesn’t do anything, that’s not you doing something wrong; just use the mouse/menu alternative that appears next to every shortcut in this lab. Every step in this lab lists both.

1.2. Why You’re Doing This Lab

Before an engineer writes a single line of real code on any given day, they:

  1. Open a project folder in their editor.

  2. Run it locally to confirm it works.

  3. Check Git to see what’s changed since their last commit.

This is the muscle memory of professional software work. Watching your own hand do it once — even on a small change — will make every future conversation with your engineers more grounded. When they say "I ran it locally and the build was green," you’ll know exactly what that looks like.

1.3. What’s Already Set Up on Your Lab VM

Your lab machine runs Ubuntu Linux and comes pre-installed with everything you need for this lab. You do not need to install anything.

  • Visual Studio Code — the code editor you will use throughout the program.

  • Git — the version control tool engineers use to track changes to code.

  • Firefox and Chrome — either browser can be used to view the Evergreen quote page.

  • A terminal application — for typing commands directly to the computer.

You will install one VS Code extension during this lab (Live Server), because it’s used only for web-focused labs. The step-by-step below covers that.

1.4. Your Lab VM Desktop — A Quick Tour

When you connect to your lab VM, you will see a desktop that looks like the screenshot below. The numbered callouts highlight the seven items you will use most in this lab.

  1. Activities menu (top-left corner) — click this if you need to find any app that is not on the dock. Typing a name in the search box filters the app list.

  2. Home folder (desktop icon) — your personal folder. Inside it you will find a Labfiles folder that holds all the starter files for every lab in this course, including evergreen-quote-first-look for this lab.

  3. LabGuides folder (desktop icon) — where the written instructions for each lab live on the VM. You can open it any time to re-read a step.

  4. Files (dock, orange folder icon) — a graphical file browser, similar to Windows File Explorer or macOS Finder. Useful for browsing folders visually instead of from the command line.

  5. Terminal (dock, black icon with >_) — the command line. You will use this to run cd, ls, git status, and code . and other commands.

  6. Chrome browser (dock, colorful circular icon) — one of the two browsers installed on the VM. Either Chrome or Firefox can be used to view the Evergreen quote page.

  7. Visual Studio Code (dock, blue angular icon) — the code editor you will use for the rest of this lab and every lab after it.

 Tip

If the dock is hidden, move your mouse to the bottom of the screen and it will reappear.

1.5. Open the Folder from the Command Line

You’ll navigate to the lab folder using the terminal, then open it in VS Code from the command line. This is how most engineers open projects — faster than clicking through a file browser.

  1. Open a terminal. Three ways — use whichever feels easiest:

    • From the dock: Click the Terminal icon in the dock at the bottom of the screen (the black icon with >_ — callout (5) on the desktop tour above).

    • From Activities: Click Activities in the top-left corner, type Terminal in the search box, then click the Terminal icon in the results.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Alt+T.

  2. Navigate into the lab folder:

    cd ~/Labfiles/evergreen-quote-first-look

     Note

    If you get "No such file or directory," run ls ~/Labfiles to see what’s actually there and adjust the path. The ~ symbol is shorthand for your home folder.

  3. Confirm you’re in the right place:

    ls

    You should see index.html, styles.css, app.js, and README.md.

    Why this step: ls is one of the commands you will see engineers use most. It answers the question "what’s in this folder?" — the terminal equivalent of opening a file browser. Every engineer starting work on a new project runs ls before they do anything else.

  4. Open the folder in VS Code from the terminal:

    code .

    The . means "the current folder." VS Code will open with this folder loaded in its sidebar.

     Note

    The first time you open a folder, VS Code may show a dialog titled "Do you trust the authors of the files in this folder?" This is a safety prompt that appears on any folder VS Code has not seen before — it’s not specific to this lab. The Evergreen files are provided by your course, so click Yes, I trust the authors. If you click No, VS Code opens the folder in a restricted mode that will prevent some of the steps later in this lab from working.

    Why this step: Engineers almost never open VS Code from the application menu and then go File → Open Folder. They open a terminal, cd into the project, and type code .. It connects the command line to the editor in one motion and is a signal of someone who has done this a thousand times.

1.6. A Quick Tour of the VS Code Window

Now that VS Code is open, take a moment to orient yourself. The numbered callouts below highlight the five areas you will use most in this lab.

  1. Menu bar (very top) — the familiar application menus: File, Edit, Selection, View, Go, Run, Terminal, Help. Later in this lab you will use File > Save to save your change and Terminal > New Terminal to open a terminal inside VS Code.

  2. Activity Bar (vertical strip of icons on the far left) — the main navigation for the sidebar. Each icon switches the sidebar to a different panel: Explorer (files), Search, Source Control (Git), Run & Debug, and Extensions. You’ll use several of these in this lab.

  3. Explorer panel (sidebar, just right of the Activity Bar) — shows the project files for the folder you opened. Under EVERGREEN-QUOTE-FIRST-LOOK you should see four files: app.js, index.html, README.md, and styles.css. Clicking a file opens it in the editor.

  4. Editor area (the large center region) — where files open for viewing and editing. Each open file becomes a tab at the top. The first time VS Code opens, it shows a Welcome tab; you can close it by clicking the X on the tab.

  5. Status bar / Go Live button (the thin bar along the very bottom, right-hand side) — you will see a Go Live button here after you install the Live Server extension in the next section. Clicking it starts a local web server and opens your app in the browser.

 Tip

VS Code has many more features than this lab will cover. You only need the five areas above today — you’ll meet more of VS Code as the weeks go on.

1.7. Explore the Project

  1. Look at the Explorer panel on the left side of the VS Code window. Two ways to open it:

    • With the mouse: Click the top icon in the Activity Bar (the vertical strip of icons on the far left). It looks like two overlapping pages.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+E.

  2. You should see four files listed in the Explorer:

    • index.html — the page structure

    • styles.css — the visual styling

    • app.js — the behavior (the premium calculation)

    • README.md — a short description of the app

  3. Click index.html in the Explorer to open it in the editor.

  4. Scroll through the file briefly. You do not need to understand the tags. Notice only the structure: there is a header, a hero section with a headline and a tagline, a form, and a footer. This is the HTML "skeleton" of every web page.

    Why this matters: When your engineers talk about "the markup" or "the HTML," this is what they mean. You will look at it more closely in HTML week (Week 3). For now, just notice that the page is readable text, not a binary file.

1.8. Install the Live Server Extension

Live Server is a VS Code extension that automatically refreshes your browser every time you save a file. You will use it to run and preview the Evergreen app.

  1. Open the Extensions panel. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the fifth icon down in the Activity Bar (it looks like four small squares with one square pulled away).

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+X.

  2. In the search box at the top, type Live Server.

  3. Find the extension named Live Server by Ritwick Dey and click the Install button next to it.

    Why this step: Extensions are small add-ons that give VS Code extra abilities. Most engineers have a handful of favorites. Installing one during the lab lets you see how the extensions system works — it’s the same process for any extension you might install later.

1.9. Run the App

  1. Go back to the Explorer panel. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the top icon in the Activity Bar (the two overlapping pages).

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+E.

  2. Right-click on index.html and choose Open with Live Server.

  3. The Evergreen quote page should open in your browser (Firefox or Chrome — either works). It should look like the screenshot below.

    Try it out: enter a name, a ZIP code, pick a coverage type, drag the slider, and click Calculate Premium. An estimated monthly premium should appear below the form.

    Why Live Server: It reloads the browser every time you save a file. You will see that in the next section. Without it, you’d have to manually refresh the browser after every change, which gets tedious fast.

  4. Leave the browser tab open. Switch back to VS Code.

1.10. Initialize Git for This Folder

Up to now, nothing has been tracking your project in version control. Git does not automatically track every folder — you have to tell Git to start tracking this one. Engineers do this once, at the start of every new project.

  1. Open the Source Control panel. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the third icon down in the Activity Bar (it looks like a branching line with dots — callout (2) from the VS Code tour above).

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+G.

  2. You should see a panel with a message explaining the folder doesn’t have a Git repository yet, along with a blue Initialize Repository button.

    The numbered callouts point to (1) the Source Control icon in the Activity Bar that you just clicked, and (2) the blue Initialize Repository button you will click in the next step.

  3. Click Initialize Repository. VS Code creates a hidden .git folder inside your project. All four of your project files will now appear in the Source Control panel under Changes with a small U next to each name. U stands for "untracked" — Git has seen the files but they aren’t part of any commit yet.

    Why this step: Every real engineering project starts the same way: initialize Git, commit the starting files once as a baseline, and from then on every future change shows up as a modification against that baseline. Without this step, Git has nothing to compare your changes against, and VS Code’s Source Control panel can’t tell you what changed.

  4. Make an initial commit so there is a baseline to compare future changes against:

    • Click inside the Message box at the top of the Source Control panel (it shows grey placeholder text like "Message (press Ctrl+Enter to commit)").

    • Type Initial commit.

    • Click the blue Commit button just below the message box. If VS Code asks "Would you like to stage all your changes and commit them directly?", click Yes.

  5. The Source Control panel should now be empty under Changes — everything has been committed. Your project has exactly one commit in its history. From this point on, any edit you make to any file will show up in the Source Control panel as a modification.

1.11. Make a One-Line Change

You are going to change the page’s tagline from Get a personalized quote in under a minute. to Get a personalized quote in under 60 seconds.

  1. Make sure index.html is the file currently open in the editor. If not, click it in the Explorer.

  2. Find the tagline line. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Use the scroll bar on the right side of the editor and scroll until you see a line that contains under a minute.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+F to open the Find box, type under a minute, and press Enter — the editor will jump to that line.

  3. Change the line so it reads:

    <p class="tagline">Get a personalized quote in under 60 seconds.</p>

  4. Save the file. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the File menu at the top of the VS Code window, then click Save.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+S.

  5. Switch to your browser tab. The page should update automatically — the tagline now reads the new text.

    Why this matters: The "edit file, save, see it update in the browser" cycle is the inner loop of front-end development. Your engineers do this hundreds of times a day. When they talk about "hot reload" or "fast feedback," this is what they mean.

1.12. See the Change in Git

VS Code has a built-in view for Git. Even though you have not run any git commands yourself, VS Code has been tracking your change.

  1. Open the Source Control panel. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the third icon down in the Activity Bar (it looks like a branching line with dots).

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+G.

  2. You should see index.html listed with an M next to it. M means "modified."

    Why this matters: This view shows the same information you would get from running git status in the terminal. Engineers use this view constantly to see exactly what they have changed before they commit. When an engineer says "let me just sanity-check my changes," this is the panel they are looking at.

  3. Click on index.html in the Source Control panel. VS Code will open a diff view showing the old line on the left (highlighted red) and your new line on the right (highlighted green).

    Why this matters: The diff view is the fundamental unit of code review. When an engineer opens a pull request, this is exactly what their teammates see. You will revisit this in Week 2 (GitHub). For now, just notice how focused it is: only what changed, nothing else.

  4. Close the diff view by clicking the X on its tab at the top of the editor.

1.13. See the Same Thing from the Command Line

You have just seen Git through VS Code’s graphical panel. Now see it through the terminal, so you know the two views show the same thing.

  1. Open VS Code’s integrated terminal. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the Terminal menu at the top of the VS Code window, then click New Terminal.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+`.

  2. The terminal opens inside your project folder. Run:

    git status

    You should see modified: index.html — the same information the Source Control panel showed you.

  3. Now run:

    git diff

    You’ll see the same before/after change — this time in text form, with red - and green + marks.

    Why this matters: The graphical and command-line views of Git are just two windows into the same thing. Some engineers prefer the terminal, others prefer the visual panel, and most switch between them. You don’t have to pick a favorite. You just have to know they’re equivalent.

1.14. Clean Up

The lab environment will be reset between sessions, but practice cleanup anyway.

  1. In the terminal, discard your change so the folder is back to its starting state:

    git checkout index.html

    Why this step: git checkout <file> throws away uncommitted changes to that file. Engineers use this when they realize they went down the wrong path and want a clean slate. Important: this deletes your work — use it only when you are sure.

  2. Confirm the change is gone:

    git status

    It should say nothing to commit, working tree clean.

1.15. Review

In this lab, you:

  • Navigated to a project folder from the command line using cd and ls.

  • Opened a folder in VS Code from the terminal using code ..

  • Installed a VS Code extension (Live Server).

  • Ran a small web app in your browser.

  • Edited a line of HTML and watched the browser hot-reload.

  • Viewed the change in VS Code’s Source Control panel.

  • Viewed the same change from the command line with git status and git diff.

  • Discarded the change with git checkout.

You have not written any code. You have watched the surface — the tools and the feedback loops — of what your engineers do every day. That context is what the rest of this program builds on.
